claims.claim_text: Unsourced 2019 fintech outage anecdote blaming Kafka on Deployments instead of StatefulSets.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | first outage paragraph
claims.anchor_text: In 2019, a major fintech company experienced a catastrophic, multi-hour outage affecting millions of transactions. The root cause? They had deployed their distributed messaging queue (Kafka) using standard Deployments rather than StatefulSets. When a brief network partition caused several nodes to drop, the Deployment controller eagerly spun up replacement pods. Because Deployments provide no guarantees regarding stable network identities or ordered startup, the new brokers came online with entirely new hostnames and immediately began attempting to form a new cluster split-brain style, utterly corrupting the topic metadata.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Using a Deployment for systems such as Kafka can be dangerous because replacement Pods do not keep the stable identities and ordered startup behavior that many clustered stateful systems expect, so a disruption can turn into membership or recovery problems.
claims.rationale: The named incident, date, and causal chain are not attributable to an allowlisted primary source and must be collapsed to the general operational lesson.

claims.claim_text: Unsourced claim that the incident cost over $4 million and a full weekend of reconciliation.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | second outage paragraph
claims.anchor_text: This incident cost the company over $4 million in SLA penalties and required a full weekend of manual data reconciliation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Misapplying workload controllers to a stateful system can turn a routine disruption into an expensive, labor-intensive recovery effort.
claims.rationale: This is fake-precise impact data attached to an unattributed anecdote, so the numbers must be removed rather than softened.

claims.claim_text: A DaemonSet ensures all or some nodes run a Pod copy, adds Pods on new nodes, garbage-collects them from removed nodes, and deleting the DaemonSet cleans up its Pods.
claims.span_hint: section: 1.1 What Is a DaemonSet?
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes DaemonSet concept page states these controller guarantees directly.

claims.claim_text: The comparison table says DaemonSet scheduling bypasses the scheduler.
claims.span_hint: section: 1.4 DaemonSet vs Deployment | comparison table
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Scheduling | Targets eligible nodes and then uses the scheduler to bind Pods | Uses scheduler |
claims.rationale: Current upstream docs say the DaemonSet controller targets eligible nodes and the default scheduler typically binds the Pods, so the row is outdated as written.

claims.claim_text: The module says DaemonSets ignore most scheduling constraints by default and run on control plane nodes unless taints stop them.
claims.span_hint: section: 1.5 DaemonSet Commands | Did You Know callout
claims.anchor_text: DaemonSets ignore most scheduling constraints by default. They even run on control plane nodes if there are no taints preventing it. Use `nodeSelector` or `tolerations` to control placement.
claims.suggested_rewrite: DaemonSets automatically get several built-in tolerations for node conditions, but normal taints and placement rules still apply; control plane nodes usually need explicit tolerations if they are tainted. Use `nodeSelector` or `tolerations` to control placement.
claims.rationale: The first two sentences overstate DaemonSet behavior and blur the distinction between built-in tolerations and ordinary taint handling.

claims.claim_text: DaemonSet supports `RollingUpdate` as the default update strategy and `OnDelete` for manual replacement updates.
claims.span_hint: section: 2.3 Update Strategy
claims.rationale: The upstream DaemonSet update guide explicitly documents both strategies and the default.

claims.claim_text: StatefulSets provide stable network identity, stable persistent storage, and ordered graceful deployment and scaling for stateful applications.
claims.span_hint: section: 3.1 What Is a StatefulSet?
claims.rationale: These are the core guarantees listed in the StatefulSet concept documentation.

claims.claim_text: StatefulSets require a headless Service to establish Pod network identity.
claims.span_hint: section: 3.3 StatefulSet Requirements
claims.rationale: The StatefulSet docs explicitly say a headless Service is required for StatefulSet Pod network identity.

claims.claim_text: StatefulSet Pod DNS follows the `<pod-name>.<service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local` pattern, such as `web-0.nginx.default.svc.cluster.local`.
claims.span_hint: section: 3.4 Stable Network Identity
claims.rationale: The StatefulSet docs describe the hostname and governing service domain pattern directly.

claims.claim_text: Each StatefulSet replica gets its own PVC from `volumeClaimTemplates`, and replacement Pods reattach to their existing storage.
claims.span_hint: section: 3.5 Stable Storage
claims.rationale: The StatefulSet concept page explains per-replica PVC creation and reattachment of existing volumes on reschedule.

claims.claim_text: The module says StatefulSet PVCs are not automatically deleted when the StatefulSet is deleted.
claims.span_hint: section: 3.5 Stable Storage | Did You Know callout
claims.anchor_text: When you delete a StatefulSet, the PVCs are NOT automatically deleted. This is a safety feature—you keep your data. To clean up, manually delete the PVCs after deleting the StatefulSet.
claims.suggested_rewrite: By default, StatefulSet PVCs are retained when the StatefulSet is deleted, but newer Kubernetes versions also support configurable PVC retention policies. Clean up retained PVCs only when you are sure the data is no longer needed.
claims.rationale: The categorical statement is outdated because Kubernetes now supports `persistentVolumeClaimRetentionPolicy`; the rewritten version preserves the main lesson without asserting an absolute.

claims.claim_text: StatefulSets create Pods sequentially from low to high ordinal, delete them in reverse order, and gate progress on predecessor readiness.
claims.span_hint: section: 4.1 Ordered Creation and Deletion
claims.rationale: The Deployment and Scaling Guarantees section in the StatefulSet docs states these ordering rules directly.

claims.claim_text: With a partitioned RollingUpdate, only Pods whose ordinal is greater than or equal to the partition are updated; `partition: 2` updates only `web-2` in a 3-replica StatefulSet.
claims.span_hint: section: 4.3 Update Strategy
claims.rationale: The StatefulSet RollingUpdate docs define partitioned updates in exactly these ordinal terms.

claims.claim_text: The comparison table says Deployment rolling updates happen in random order.
claims.span_hint: section: 5.1 Comparison | comparison table
claims.anchor_text: | Rolling update | Random order | Reverse order (N-1 first) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Rolling update | No stable ordinal update order guarantee | Reverse ordinal order (N-1 first) |
claims.rationale: Deployment Pods do not have StatefulSet-style ordinals, but calling the update order simply random is imprecise and not the upstream guarantee.

claims.claim_text: Unsourced PostgreSQL outage war story used to argue for StatefulSets.
claims.span_hint: section: 5.2 When to Use What | War Story callout
claims.anchor_text: A team deployed PostgreSQL using a Deployment with a PVC. It worked—until the pod was rescheduled. The new pod got a different IP, replication broke, and the standby couldn't find the primary. Switching to a StatefulSet with stable network identity fixed everything. Use the right tool!
claims.suggested_rewrite: Using a Deployment for a database that depends on per-replica identity can cause reconnection or replication problems after rescheduling; a StatefulSet is the safer default when the application needs stable network identity.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed anecdote with a specific causal chain, so it should be rewritten to the general lesson rather than cited.

claims.claim_text: A headless Service uses `clusterIP: None`, does not get a virtual IP or kube-proxy load balancing, and exposes backing Pod IPs through DNS.
claims.span_hint: section: 6.1 What Is a Headless Service?
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes Service docs define headless Services in these exact terms.

claims.claim_text: The module says headless-Service lookups return IPs in randomized order for client-side load balancing.
claims.span_hint: section: 6.2 DNS Resolution Comparison | Did You Know callout
claims.anchor_text: CoreDNS automatically creates multiple A records for a headless service. If your StatefulSet has 5 replicas, doing a DNS lookup on the headless service name returns 5 different IP addresses in a randomized order, enabling basic client-side load balancing!
claims.suggested_rewrite: For a headless Service, cluster DNS returns the set of backing Pod IPs rather than a single virtual IP, so clients can connect directly to individual replicas or apply their own selection logic.
claims.rationale: The upstream docs guarantee a set of Pod IPs, but not the exact randomized-order phrasing used here.

claims.claim_text: The module says there is no native way to start StatefulSet ordinals at 1 or another index.
claims.span_hint: section: 6.2 DNS Resolution Comparison | second Did You Know callout
claims.anchor_text: There is no native configuration to start the index at 1 or skip numbers.
claims.suggested_rewrite: By default, StatefulSet ordinals start at 0, and newer Kubernetes versions also let you set a custom start ordinal with `.spec.ordinals.start`; ordinals still remain sequential.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes now supports `.spec.ordinals.start`, so the original sentence is false as written.

claims.claim_text: The common-mistakes table says mismatched DaemonSet labels cause infinite Pod creation.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes | last table row
claims.anchor_text: | Forgetting to match labels in DaemonSet | DaemonSet controller creates infinite pods or fails | Ensure `spec.selector.matchLabels` matches `template.metadata.labels` |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Forgetting to match labels in DaemonSet | The API rejects the DaemonSet because the selector must match the pod template labels | Ensure `spec.selector.matchLabels` matches `template.metadata.labels` |
claims.rationale: Current Kubernetes validation rejects selector/template mismatches, so the 'infinite pods' wording is misleading.

claims.claim_text: Quiz answer claims DaemonSets can tolerate taints that normal Deployments cannot.
claims.span_hint: section: Quiz | answer 1
claims.anchor_text: Additionally, DaemonSets can tolerate taints that normal Deployments cannot, ensuring coverage on special-purpose nodes like GPU nodes or control plane nodes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Additionally, DaemonSets often ship with or are configured with the tolerations needed for node-level agents, but Deployments can also tolerate taints when you explicitly add matching tolerations.
claims.rationale: Tolerations are not unique to DaemonSets; the current sentence incorrectly frames them as a capability Deployments lack.

further_reading.title: DaemonSet
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream reference for DaemonSet guarantees, scheduling behavior, tolerations, and node coverage.

further_reading.title: StatefulSets
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream reference for StatefulSet identity, storage, ordering, update strategy, and PVC retention behavior.

further_reading.title: DNS for Services and Pods
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains how normal and headless Services resolve in cluster DNS, including per-Pod records used by StatefulSets.

notes: Quiz answers 2, 3, 6, and 7 repeat earlier concepts about StatefulSet identity, PVC retention, control-plane taints, and headless-Service DNS; reuse the same upstream sources if citations are also injected into those answer blocks.
